rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is an over-triage-resistance case. The presentation is alarming (feverishness, total-body stiffness, bedbound, forefoot pain, an urgent after-hours call framed to exclude sepsis/septic arthritis), but on patient-reportable features the acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE: symmetrical bilateral small-joint involvement of both hands and feet, prolonged morning stiffness, only subjective low-grade feverishness with no rigors or measured high fever, and no single disproportionately hot/red/swollen joint, on a background of known seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on hydroxychloroquine. The correct disposition is T2 - prompt (same-day to 24-48h) IN-PERSON GP assessment to examine the joints, screen for infection and decide on a steroid, with expedited rheumatology review - NOT emergency escalation. The in-person requirement is because telehealth cannot examine a joint or exclude a septic one, and steroids must not be started blindly; it is prompt review, not an ambulance. Prominent mandatory safety-netting escalates to T4/T5 if a septic-joint or sepsis red flag appears.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline disposition: severe symmetrical polyarthritis flare of known RA with feverishness but a negative acute red-flag screen
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Urgent in-person GP assessment today or within 24-48 hours; rheumatology review within about 5 days
escalation_edges.rationale: Prompt hands-on review is needed to examine the joints, exclude a septic joint and start appropriate flare treatment; this does not require emergency escalation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This looks like a flare of your rheumatoid arthritis. Because you feel feverish, you need to be seen and examined in person today or tomorrow before starting any steroid - I'll help arrange an urgent GP appointment and a rheumatology review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single joint becomes disproportionately swollen, bright red, hot and severely painful
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Present to the emergency department the same day, without delay
escalation_edges.rationale: A monoarticular hot joint must be assumed to be septic arthritis until proven otherwise by aspiration - a joint-destroying emergency requiring urgent hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any single joint suddenly becomes much more swollen, bright red and hot than the others, go to the emergency department straight away - that needs urgent tests to rule out a joint infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever above 38.5C with rigors / uncontrollable shivering, or feeling very unwell, confused or faint
escalation_edges.rationale: A high spiking fever with rigors on this background raises systemic sepsis, which is life-threatening and time-critical.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever with uncontrollable shivering, or feel very unwell, confused or faint, call 000 for an ambulance - that could be a serious infection in the blood.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A fall, or being unable to move safely / manage at home even with the spouse's help
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent same-day in-person help (urgent GP, urgent care, or ED if injured)
escalation_edges.rationale: The high falls risk and functional collapse are a genuine safety issue; a fall or inability to cope safely warrants urgent same-day in-person assessment and support.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you have a fall, or you and your spouse can't manage safely at home, get urgent same-day help - and go to the emergency department if you're hurt in a fall.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Bedbound and fully dependent on an anxious spouse for all transfers and care
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Factor the carer's capacity into the plan; a same-day in-person review (rather than 24-48h) is reasonable given the functional collapse and carer strain, and mobility/OT support should be expedited. This raises urgency within T2 toward same-day, not to emergency escalation.
Immunomodulation can blunt the signs of infection, so keep the septic-joint and sepsis safety-net prominent and lower the threshold to escalate if any red flag appears.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is most likely a flare of your known rheumatoid arthritis. You need an urgent in-person doctor review today or within the next day or two to examine your joints, check for infection and decide on treatment before any steroid is started - keep taking your usual hydroxychloroquine in the meantime, rest the joints, use supportive shoes and let your spouse help you move so you don't fall.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent in-person GP appointment (or home visit / urgent care) arranged today; expedited rheumatology review
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours GP or home-visit service for the flare; but if a single joint turns hot and red, or you get a high fever with shaking chills, do not wait - go to the emergency department or call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the reviewing GP and rheumatologist with the flare history, the negative acute red-flag screen (symmetrical involvement, no single hot joint, low-grade feverishness, no rigors), current medications, and the safety-net advice given.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot examine the joints or measure vitals, so a septic joint cannot be definitively excluded remotely even though the reported pattern favours a flare.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the most likely cause (RA flare), keep septic arthritis explicitly on the list, arrange prompt in-person assessment to resolve the uncertainty, and give clear red-flag safety-netting - do not start steroids blindly.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either over-escalating the whole picture to an ambulance/ED because of the feverish framing, or falsely reassuring the patient it is 'just a flare' and omitting the septic-joint safety-net.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating it as pure self-care / routine review with no infection safety-net, missing the possibility of a septic joint or sepsis in an immunomodulated RA patient with feverishness.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving septic joint or systemic sepsis is not escalated, leading to joint destruction, bacteraemia or death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Correct tier but delivered without the mandatory single-hot-joint and high-fever-with-rigors safety-net, or endorsing remote steroid initiation - the patient may start immunosuppression or fail to recognise a developing infection because the escalation triggers were not communicated.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending the whole presentation straight to ED or calling an ambulance despite a negative acute red-flag screen - the scored defect this case is designed to test; causes an unnecessary, painful ambulance transfer and ED attendance for a flare that prompt in-person GP review manages appropriately.
